author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
God’s Agency is Needful Even in Man’s Doings.
title: God’s Agency is Needful Even in Man’s Doings.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For as it is said, “It is man’s part to prepare his heart, and the answer of the tongue is from the Lord;” so also is it said, “Open thy mouth, and I will fill it.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For although, save by His assistance without whom we can do nothing, we cannot open our mouth, yet we open it by His aid and by our own agency, while the Lord fills it without our agency.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For what is to prepare the heart and to open the mouth, but to prepare the will?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: And yet in the same scriptures is read, “The will is prepared by the Lord,” and, “Thou shalt open my lips, and my mouth shall show forth Thy praise.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: So God admonishes us to prepare our will in what we read,” It is man’s part to prepare his heart;” and yet, that man may do this, God helps him, because the will is prepared by the Lord.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: This He so says by way of command, as that nobody can do this unless it is done by His aid, to whom it is said, “Thou shalt open my lips.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Are any of these men so foolish as to contend that the mouth is one thing, the lips another; and to say with marvellous triviality that man opens his own mouth, and God opens man’s lips?

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: And yet God restrains them from even that absurdity where He says to Moses His servant, “I will open thy mouth, and I will instruct thee what thou oughtest to speak.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: In that clause, therefore, where He says, “Open thy mouth and I will fill it,” it seems, as it were, that one of them pertains to man, the other to God.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: But in this, where it is said, “I will open thy mouth and will instruct thee,” both belong to God.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Why is this, except that in one of these cases He co-operates with man as the agent, in the other He does it alone?
